data.title: Super Micro Co-Founder Arrested in Alleged $2.5B AI Chip Smuggling Scheme to China
data.description:
U.S. authorities have arrested Yih-Shyan Liaw, co-founder of Super Micro Computer, Inc., in connection with an alleged multibillion-dollar scheme to circumvent export controls and funnel advanced artificial intelligence servers to China, marking one of the most significant enforcement actions in the ongoing crackdown on sensitive chip transfers.

🚨BREAKING: SUPER MICRO CO-FOUNDER ARRESTED FOR SMUGGLING $2.5B IN NVIDIA GPUs TO CHINA

>SMCI co-founder Yih-Shyan "Wally" Liaw arrested today
>personally holds $464 MILLION in SMCI stock
>charged with smuggling BILLIONS in Nvidia servers to china
>used a southeast asian shell… https://t.co/SfIFc0SPedpic.twitter.com/93QE72ddph

— NIK (@ns123abc) March 20, 2026

The U.S. Department of Justice unsealed an indictment charging Liaw, along with sales executives Ruei-Tsang “Steven” Chang and Ting-Wei “Willy” Sun, with conspiring to export approximately $2.5 billion worth of servers embedded with restricted graphics processing units to Chinese buyers between 2024 and 2025.

Prosecutors allege the operation relied on falsified documentation, shell entities and staged compliance measures to obscure the true destination of the hardware.

The scale of the activity drew particular scrutiny, with authorities highlighting that more than $510 million in shipments were executed within a two-month window between April and May 2025, underscoring both the speed and coordination of the alleged network.

Concealment network designed to bypass export restrictions

Investigators describe a layered transshipment strategy aimed at evading U.S. export controls on advanced computing hardware. According to the indictment, the defendants allegedly fabricated records, staged non-functional equipment to satisfy audit requirements and routed transactions through intermediary entities to conceal end users.

The servers, built using high-performance components from partners such as Nvidia and Google, were reportedly redirected through third-party jurisdictions before reaching Chinese clients. The use of pass-through companies and falsified inventories allowed the operation to present an appearance of compliance while masking its underlying intent.

这个事情给nvidia 芯片掮客的一记警钟

今天凌晨，Supermicro 的联创 Yih-Shyan "Wally" Liaw（及2同伙）被抓了。。。（原籍应该是台湾）

事情是因为Wally被指控涉嫌把nvidia的芯片（25亿美金）通过东南亚某国的空壳公司然后转口到中国。… https://t.co/zFaltroZZBpic.twitter.com/wMpvZbPSlN

— danny (@agintender) March 20, 2026

Liaw and Sun have been taken into custody and are expected to appear in the Northern District of California, while Chang, identified as a Taiwanese national, remains at large.

The charges reflect a coordinated effort, according to authorities, to exploit gaps in oversight as demand for AI infrastructure accelerates globally.

Company distances itself as market reaction turns negative

Super Micro Computer, Inc. has not been named as a defendant in the case and has moved to distance itself from the alleged misconduct, stating that the actions described by prosecutors run counter to its internal compliance policies. The company added that it is cooperating fully with the investigation.

Investor sentiment shifted sharply following the announcement. After initially trading higher during the session, Super Micro shares fell more than 13% in after-hours trading, reflecting heightened concern over regulatory exposure and reputational risk.

Super Micro $SMCI is now down almost 15% in after hours following its earnings pic.twitter.com/fz7xibx9lc

— Evan (@StockMKTNewz) April 30, 2024

The case highlights intensifying U.S. scrutiny over the global flow of advanced computing technology, particularly as artificial intelligence infrastructure becomes a strategic asset.

Export controls targeting high-performance chips — many of which are central to data center training and large-scale AI models — have become a focal point in broader geopolitical competition.

By pursuing criminal charges tied to large-scale evasion, authorities are signaling a more aggressive enforcement posture aimed at closing loopholes in complex international supply chains.

*TRUMP'S 10% CHINA TARIFF SET TO TAKE EFFECT AS DEADLINE PASSES

*CHINA TO LEVY TAX ON SOME PRODUCTS IMPORTED FROM US

*CHINA PROBES GOOGLE OVER ALLEGED ANTI-TRUST LAW BREACHES: SAMR

*CHINA SETS EXPORT CONTROL ON TUNGSTEN-RELATED MATERIALS

— Jim Bianco (@biancoresearch) February 4, 2025

At the same time, the case underscores the difficulty of policing cross-border technology flows, where intermediaries and layered transactions can obscure accountability.

As global demand for AI hardware continues to surge, the outcome of the case is likely to shape both compliance expectations and enforcement priorities, with implications extending beyond a single company to the wider semiconductor and data infrastructure ecosystem.

data.title: The sins and non-sinfulness of buying and selling USDT: How can ordinary people use USDT "painlessly"?
data.description:
Since the joint issuance of the "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currency" (hereinafter referred to as Notice No. 2.6 or Document No. 42) by eight ministries and commissions in 2026, the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate have held meetings at various levels on multiple occasions, publicly stating that they will increase the investigation and punishment of illegal foreign exchange and money laundering using cryptocurrencies. For example, at a recent important meeting, Supreme People's Court President Zhang Jun clearly pointed out in his work report: "Punish new types of crimes according to law, severely punish crimes such as money laundering and foreign exchange evasion using virtual currencies as a medium, and work together to prevent illegal cross-border fund transfers to safeguard national financial security and social stability." Based on the practical experience of the Sa Jie team, although crypto assets issued on public blockchains generally possess technical characteristics such as peer-to-peer global transmission and anonymous transactions, BTC and ETH, due to their highly volatile value, are positioned more as investment products than value tools. Stablecoins like USDT and USDC, "pegged" to the US dollar, have become a more liquid and widely used payment and settlement tool. Today, the Sa Jie team will use several typical cases to discuss how ordinary people can use USDT "painlessly" in the current situation, solving necessary life and work needs while staying away from red-line risks. I. Crimes Involving Foreign Exchange: Frequent and Prosperous Illegal Business Operations Illegal foreign exchange trading is a highly unique crime in my country's criminal law system. It originates from the only existing single-subject criminal law in my country—the "Decision on Punishing Crimes of Fraudulently Purchasing Foreign Exchange and Evading Foreign Exchange Controls," promulgated by the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress on December 29, 1998. This currently effective single-subject criminal law has withstood decades of changes and twelve amendments to the Criminal Law, remaining in effect to this day, demonstrating the special nature of this crime. In 2019, the Supreme People's Court issued the "Interpretation on Several Issues Concerning the Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Illegally Engaging in Fund Payment and Settlement Business and Illegally Buying and Selling Foreign Exchange," which refined a series of practical standards for the crime of illegal business operations involving the illegal buying and selling of foreign exchange, and clarified the specific amounts for "serious circumstances" and "particularly serious circumstances." Since then, the aforementioned normative document has become the basis for convicting and punishing business activities involving the exchange of crypto assets for fiat currency as illegal business operations. However, it is worth noting that to this day, there is still no clear law, regulation, or normative document defining "using crypto assets to exchange for currency as illegal buying and selling of foreign exchange." In judicial practice, guidance for judgments at the grassroots level is mainly provided through a series of reference cases in the Supreme People's Court's case database, primarily the case of Wan Mouyuan et al. (database entry number: 2025-18-1-085-001). As a result, among crimes involving U.S. currencies, the crime of illegal foreign exchange trading has become the most frequent and prevalent criminal red line risk. II. Using U.S. currencies to assist foreign underground banks in currency exchange and arbitrage is a "crossing the line" behavior and should never be done. Recently, the Supreme People's Court's case database published a reference case: the case of Yan Moukang et al. for illegal business operations (database entry number: 2025-03-1-169-002). In this case, the defendant Yan Moukang met A, a Nigerian who specialized in exchanging Tether (USDT) for RMB, online. A claimed that the fees for buying and selling foreign exchange at banks or foreign exchange companies were high and wanted to exchange Nigerian Naira for RMB through Yan Moukang. The two parties then conspired to use virtual currency as a medium of exchange to circumvent foreign exchange regulations. From September 2020 to April 2021, Yan Moukang, along with defendants Lin Moucheng and Xie Mouwei, quoted A a price that was 1 or 2 cents lower than the daily exchange rate of USDT to RMB offered by virtual currency exchange dealers. After A agreed to the price, A transferred USDT purchased with his local currency, Naira, to a virtual currency exchange account controlled by Yan Moukang and others. Yan Moukang and others then sold the USDT to convert it into RMB (withdrawal), and, according to A's instructions, paid the RMB to a designated bank account in China. From September to December 2020, Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng jointly received 3,275,396.3 USDT from A Mou and sold them to domestic currency dealers, totaling RMB 21,293,872.33, with a profit of at least RMB 32,753.96. From January to April 2021, Yan Moukang, Lin Moucheng, and Xie Mouwei jointly received 1,290,342.1 USDT from A Mou and sold them to domestic currency dealers, totaling RMB 8,380,182.78, with a profit of at least RMB 12,903.42. Ultimately, although Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng's profits were relatively low and they had already returned the stolen goods before the judgment, they were still sentenced to five years in prison for illegal business operations. This case is a typical example of using personal encrypted asset accounts to help overseas underground banks bypass my country's foreign exchange controls and carry out illegal currency exchange. Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng were clearly aware that A was engaged in foreign exchange exchange business. Their main purpose in helping A withdraw U, which was exchanged using foreign legal tender, was very clear: to bypass foreign exchange regulatory authorities and avoid paying relevant handling fees. From this case, it is clear that Yan Moukang and Lin Moucheng's actions were extremely counterproductive. In a seven-month-long "arbitrage" scheme, they only profited a little over 40,000 yuan, a profit that is probably less than that of the breakfast shop selling pancakes downstairs, yet they paid the heavy price of five years in prison.

III. Cryptocurrency dealers fabricating transactions to conceal foreign exchange transactions is a "crossing the line" behavior and should never be tolerated

The case of Wan Mouyuan et al.'s illegal business operations mentioned above (entry number: 2025-18-1-085-001) is a well-known typical case of using virtual currency as a medium to buy and sell foreign exchange, constituting the crime of illegal business operations. In judicial practice, there is no doubt that such cryptocurrency dealers who use U as a medium for exchanging US dollars and RMB to evade foreign exchange supervision should be prosecuted for the crime of illegal business operations. However, the Sa Jie team needs to solemnly remind our partners that such behavior is often accompanied by false trade, and such behavior will breed more criminal risks.

Taking the case of Wan Mouyuan and others' illegal business operations as an example, after the transaction was agreed upon or the exchanged US dollars were received, the customers would typically transfer the RMB used to purchase the US dollars to a bank account provided by Wan Mouyuan (in this case, there was a mix of legal tender and US dollars). At this point, Wan Mouyuan would usually collect the RMB payment from the customers through fictitious transactions, using the method of payment for goods, thus completing the transaction. During this process, in order to comply with the bank's foreign exchange settlement requirements, Wan Mouyuan forged a large number of false transaction materials. Even to achieve the requirement of consistency between the contract, funds, and invoices, he issued VAT invoices to customers through entities he registered or controlled. Therefore, Wan Mouyuan's act of fabricating transactions to conceal foreign exchange transactions actually constitutes not only the crime of illegal business operations but also the crime of issuing false VAT invoices (dealt with in another case), and he is very likely to be punished for multiple crimes. IV. How can ordinary people use U "painlessly"? Avoiding "business activities" is key. In practice, Chinese judicial organs generally focus on the following situations to determine whether a party's behavior constitutes "business activity." (I) Whether there is a profit-making purpose. Profit-making purpose is the essential characteristic of business activities. Generally, as long as the actor subjectively intends to obtain economic benefits through transactions, regardless of whether actual profit is made, as long as there is an active pursuit of profit or indirect avoidance of loss, it can be determined that there is a profit-making purpose. For example, in the illegal business cases of Yan Moukang et al. and Wan Mouyuan et al., the defendants all "took a cut" in the relevant transactions or earned a certain price difference by adjusting the transaction price and thus profited. Actions not intended for profit, such as individuals using USDT to purchase consumer goods, charitable acts (such as non-profit organizations providing free education or charitable services), or acts solely intended for illegal possession (such as fraud or theft), do not constitute business activities. (II) Whether there is market transaction activity. The so-called "market transaction" activity mainly depends on whether the relevant transaction occurs between equal market entities, whether it involves the exchange of goods or the provision of services, whether there is business promotion to unspecified or potential trading partners, and whether the trading partners (goods/services) have open market liquidity (it cannot be a specific, closed transaction between individuals). (III) Whether it possesses continuity and repetition. Continuity and repetition are important characteristics of business activities. The actor does not conduct transactions accidentally or in a single instance, but engages in similar business activities in a continuous and repetitive manner. For example, in the cases of illegal business operations by Yan Moukang et al. and Wan Mouyuan et al., the parties completed dozens or even hundreds of foreign exchange transactions within a few months, demonstrating obvious continuity and repetition. In conclusion, under the current high-pressure regulatory and judicial crackdown, USDT is no longer simply a "digital token," but a core risk point in cross-border capital and foreign exchange regulation. For ordinary people, the safest way to use USDT "painlessly" is only one: stick to personal use, stay away from commercial transactions, refuse intermediaries, and avoid currency exchange.

data.title: A hairdryer uncovered the world's largest Nvidia chip smuggling case.
data.description:
Author: David｜Deep Tide TechFlow

Early this morning, Wally Liaw, co-founder of Supermicro, was arrested in California.

Supermicro is one of the world's largest AI server manufacturers and a core supply chain partner of Nvidia. Last year, it secured $13 billion in orders for Nvidia's Blackwell chips in just one quarter.

Liaw has been with the company since its founding in 1993 and is currently its Senior Vice President and a member of the Board of Directors.

He is charged with smuggling $2.5 billion worth of Nvidia AI chip servers into China through shell companies in Southeast Asia. The maximum sentence is 20 years in prison. The U.S. Department of Justice has characterized this case as the largest AI chip smuggling case in history. However, the details of the case are very much like a scene from a movie. According to the indictment, Liaw and his accomplices arranged for a Southeast Asian company to act as a "front," purchasing large quantities of servers equipped with Nvidia GPUs from Supermicro under the guise of personal use. After the servers arrived in Southeast Asia, staff removed the original packaging, packed them into unmarked boxes, and shipped them to China. The real servers were shipped away, but Supermicro's compliance team and the U.S. Department of Commerce regularly inspected the warehouse and scanned serial numbers; the warehouse couldn't be empty. So they manufactured tens of thousands of fake servers. The outer casings were identical to the real ones, but they contained no chips and couldn't be powered on; they were specifically displayed in the warehouse for auditors to see. Having fake servers wasn't enough; the serial numbers on the packaging also had to match. Workers used a hairdryer to heat the serial number sticker on the genuine product's packaging, peeled it off completely, and then stuck it onto the box of the fake server... Auditors scanned the code, and the system showed everything was normal. The factory's surveillance cameras captured that scene of the hairdryer being used. But the genuine product was already on its way to China. Even more outrageous, on one occasion, when Supermicro's own compliance team came to investigate, Sun, an accomplice, took photos and videos of fake servers and sent them to the auditors to prove that "the goods were all there." On another occasion, export control officials from the U.S. Department of Commerce personally came to inspect. Someone on site directly impersonated a lawyer to receive them... The main culprit, Liaw, is 71 years old and holds $430 million worth of Supermicro stock. Logically, he shouldn't be short of money. There's another detail in the indictment: a broker forwarded Liaw a news link about several Chinese people being arrested in the U.S. for smuggling AI chips. Liaw's reply was a string of crying face emojis, and then he continued. Following the announcement, Supermicro's stock fell 13% in after-hours trading. The company stated that Liaw had been suspended, Sun, the person involved, had been dismissed, and Steven Chang, the sales manager of the Taiwan office, was currently at large. Supermicro was not listed as a defendant. The company stated that it has a "comprehensive compliance system." However, this is not the first time this company's compliance system has run into problems. $2.5 Billion: A Chip Smuggling Production Line This smuggling chain wasn't built overnight. The indictment shows that Liaw was very proactive in orchestrating the operation. He directly used encrypted communication tools to ask the heads of Southeast Asian companies: How much can you eat in January? February? March? April? Just give a rough estimate, and then we'll use that number to apply for chip quotas from Nvidia. So the process was: First, confirm the demand from Chinese buyers, then use that quantity to negotiate quotas with Nvidia, arguing that the Southeast Asian companies were "for their own use." Nvidia approved the quota, the servers were assembled in the US, shipped to Taiwan, and then handed over to a Southeast Asian company. The Southeast Asian company unpacked them and shipped them to China. From customers to suppliers to logistics, Liaw single-handedly streamlined the entire chain. He wasn't a middleman; he was the co-founder and head of business development at Supermicro. Using his own company's supply chain, he smuggled his own company's products. Between 2024 and 2025, servers worth $2.5 billion flowed out through this channel. The indictment states that this scale gradually expanded, using the phrase "becoming increasingly audacious." The most audacious transaction occurred between late April and mid-May 2025. In three weeks, $510 million worth of servers were transshipped from the United States to China via Southeast Asia. Why the sudden acceleration? Possibly because Liaw saw a White House statement. In early 2025, the US government announced a new export control rule for AI products, expected to take effect in mid-May, further tightening the review of chip exports to Southeast Asia and other regions. Liaw forwarded this message to the head of the Southeast Asian company, adding the comment: "We need to speed these up before May 13!" May 13th is the effective date of the new regulations. Before then, existing vulnerabilities are still usable. So the last three weeks became a race against time. What normally takes months to complete was compressed into twenty days. Meanwhile, Steven Chang in the Taiwan office was responsible for another task: ensuring no one actually audited. The indictment states that he prevented auditors from inspecting the actual storage areas of the servers in the Southeast Asian data center and specifically assigned what he called a "friendly" auditor to conduct the review. All the forged documents, fake servers, and labels on the hairdryers were designed to prevent the production line from being disrupted. The production line reached its peak speed in the last three weeks. But Supermicro wasn't the only one involved in chip smuggling. Over the past two years, the U.S. Department of Justice has dismantled several chip smuggling networks. These networks ranged in size from tens of millions to hundreds of millions of dollars, with increasingly outrageous methods. The identities of the participants also evolved. The stricter the chip ban, the more respectable the smugglers become. Supermicro wasn't the first to be caught. Looking back two years, the chip smuggling business has undergone three generations of evolution. The identities of the participants have upgraded, the methods have become more and more like legitimate business, and the scale has grown larger and larger. The first generation was "ant-like" smuggling. When the chip ban was first introduced in 2022, smuggling was still physically demanding. International students, tourists, and personal shoppers would carry one or two Nvidia GPUs at a time, hiding them in their personal luggage to cross customs, claiming they were for personal use if stopped. Some people stuffed chips into boxes containing live lobsters, while others strapped them to their bodies and concealed them with fake pregnant bellies. Once in Shenzhen's Huaqiangbei, these chips have a complete after-sales industry chain. There are specialized workshops that repair faulty and disassembled chips, processing over 500 chips per month, charging $1400 to $2800 per chip. After repair, they are sold to data centers and AI startups. At this stage, smugglers have no company, no documents; they rely on human flesh and audacity. The amount involved in each transaction is small, but every little bit helps.

The second generation started using companies, engaging in deceptive practices.

In late 2025, the U.S. Department of Justice dismantled a smuggling network codenamed "Operation Gatekeeper." The mastermind was a 43-year-old man named Alan Hsu, living in Houston. He used a company under his name to purchase Nvidia chips in bulk from Lenovo and ship them to a warehouse in New Jersey. The company, Janford Realtor, is registered as a real estate company. It has never conducted a single real estate transaction. After the chips arrived at the warehouse, workers removed the original Nvidia labels and replaced them with the name of a fictitious company—"SANDKYAN." They were then transshipped through Malaysia and Thailand before finally entering China. The entire network shipped over $160 million worth of chips in eight months. The most dramatic moment in the case occurred during the raid. FBI agents infiltrated the New Jersey warehouse beforehand and secretly removed all the GPUs. The smugglers, discovering the goods were missing, assumed they had been stolen and sent people to the warehouse to "redeem" them. The ransom was negotiated at $1 million, and the person who came to make the contact was caught on the spot by the FBI. The third generation is like Supermicro, a company with specialized expertise. No longer a fake company, no longer a middleman, it's personally managed by the co-founder of a listed company. No need to buy chips from others; their own company is Nvidia's core supplier. No need to forge buyer identities; they use their own supply chain, their own customer relationships, and their own quotas to apply for chips, and then bypass their own compliance team internally. From small-scale operations to fake real estate companies, and then to executives of Silicon Valley listed companies. All three generations of smugglers share one thing in common: The end buyers are all in China, and the chips are all from Nvidia. The difference lies in the fact that those who crossed customs with backpacks in 2022 were gambling with their freedom. Those who are operating from Silicon Valley offices in 2026 are gambling with a company worth tens of billions of dollars. With each escalation of the ban, the profits from smuggling jump, and the social standing of those willing to participate also rises. So, just how profitable is this business? The Black Market Price of a Chip Why can't chip smuggling be stopped? The answer lies in the price. According to multiple overseas media reports, Nvidia's high-end GPUs are priced about 50% higher on the Chinese black market than through official channels in the US. The ban created a price difference, the price difference created profits, and profits created smugglers. Demand is inelastic. Ray Wang, an analyst at semiconductor analysis firm SemiAnalysis, told CNBC: More than 60% of China's leading AI models are still running on Nvidia hardware. Training a large model requires tens of thousands of high-end GPUs; without even one, the entire process cannot begin. This means that Chinese AI companies don't just "want to buy" Nvidia chips; they "have to buy them." [Image source: GamersNexus In-depth Investigation of Huaqiangbei Chip Market] What about the supply side? After chips leave Nvidia's factory, they pass through distributors, system integrators, and server manufacturers, each a potential entry point for smuggling. US export controls primarily restrict access at the sales and shipping stage, relying on buyers to declare their end-users. Once the chips leave the US, subsequent stages largely depend on self-regulation. A Financial Times report last year estimated that in the three months following April 2025 alone, over $1 billion worth of embargoed chips would flow into China through various channels. Industry insiders estimate the entire black market's monthly turnover could be in the billions of dollars... Ironically, the US government's own policies are also wavering. Last December, the Department of Justice announced with great fanfare that it had dismantled the Operation Gatekeeper smuggling network and arrested several suspects. However, on the same day, Trump announced on Truth Social that Nvidia would be allowed to sell H200 chips to China. Later, the US government opened another loophole, allowing Nvidia to sell lower-performance H20 chips to China, on the condition that Nvidia pay 15% of its sales revenue to the US government. This simultaneously cracking down on smuggling, selling the chips themselves, and taking a cut makes the entire ban system very difficult to explain. If you tell a chip broker that smuggling is illegal, he might reply: "The government itself sells them too, they just call it 'conditional export.'" The deeper paradox lies in the fact that Nvidia is currently the world's most valuable company, with a market capitalization of $4.3 trillion. The more advanced its chips, the greater the demand, the higher the black market premium, and the more lucrative the smuggling profits. A hairdryer can't blow away the demand. The government itself is cracking down on smuggling while simultaneously approving exports. What signal do people within the chip industry's upstream and downstream companies see? The protagonist of this article, Supermicro, also helped Musk build the Colossus AI computing cluster in 122 days and secured a $13 billion order from Nvidia's Blackwell last quarter. The people who built the servers and those who smuggled them were the same group. A $2.5 billion smuggling case ultimately came to light because of a hair dryer. Surveillance footage showed workers using hair dryers to tear off labels, and it's now on the US Department of Justice's website, visible to anyone. In the same week that Liaw was arrested, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang publicly stated that the company was about to face a $1 trillion backlog of chip orders. A trillion dollars in demand is there, and so is the black market premium. Perhaps the next person willing to pick up a hairdryer won't be far off.

data.title: Crypto fanatics in prison and the 30 billion illusion of the AI ​​era
data.description:
That crypto scammer who misappropriated massive amounts of client funds almost became the most successful investor in the AI ​​era.

Imagine the time is April 2022.

ChatGPT hadn't even been born yet, and the Cambrian explosion of AI was still more than half a year away. Throughout Silicon Valley, there were still more voices talking about the metaverse and Web3 than about grand models.

An AI security startup called Anthropic is currently raising Series B funding.

At that moment, a man approached and handed over a check. Face value: $500 million. He swallowed 86% of this funding round, raising the valuation of a company with zero annual revenue and a bleak future to nearly $6 billion. In the prehistoric winter of AI, this was nothing short of a crazy gamble. The person who handed over the check was Sam Bankman-Fried, the man known as SBF with the afro. At that time, he was the emperor of the cryptocurrency exchange FTX, basking in the glory of being the king of cryptocurrencies. The person who accepted the check was Dario Amodei, co-founder of Anthropic. Today, we know the outcome of two things: First, the $500 million was deposits from FTX users, and SBF was imprisoned for 25 years for fraud. Second, Anthropic has become one of the most important AI companies. In February 2026, it completed a $30 billion Series G funding round at a valuation of $380 billion. Amazon, Google, Nvidia, Microsoft… giants lined up to invest in it. If that money were clean, and if SBF hadn't gone to jail, the approximately 8% stake he acquired for $500 million would be worth over $30 billion today. A 60-fold return—a figure worthy of legendary status in venture capital history. A question naturally arises: how did a crypto gambler manage to find the most dazzling jewel in the crown of AI before everyone else could even see the way forward? The answer is: He didn't find it by chance; he was brought to the door by the "circle." You might not know that the SBF and Anthropic teams belong to the same niche elite circle—Effective Altruism (EA). This circle doesn't value personal relationships; it only focuses on creating long-term value with maximum efficiency. SBF believes in radical altruism that involves making money and donating it, while Anthropic is dedicated to creating safe and controllable AI. Their underlying logic is completely aligned. At that time, Anthropic lacked funds and resources, but it possessed the core team that had left OpenAI and was committed to long-term research and development in AI security. SBF, on the other hand, had money and influence and urgently needed to invest in core technologies to shed the speculative label of crypto capital and solidify its elite image. Beyond the resonance within specific circles, this significant investment was also a well-thought-out strategic move to secure a foothold in the ecosystem. In SBF's business blueprint, crypto was never the end goal. He had long recognized that simply speculating on cryptocurrencies and operating exchanges was merely a zero-sum game. The power to define the next generation of the internet would undoubtedly lie in the hands of those who integrate AI and Web3. AI is the engine of productivity, and Web3 is the underlying layer of value distribution; only by binding the two together can a closed-loop ecosystem be built. In 2022, the AI ​​sector was still in the darkness before dawn, and traditional capital dared not invest heavily. SBF seized the opportunity, investing $500 million to lock in leading companies in the AI ​​security sector, preemptively securing a core position in the future digital ecosystem. True capital investment is about betting on the sector ten years from now, not on immediate profits. Even at the peak of a bull market, SBF is well aware of the crypto industry's fatal flaws: regulatory oversight is looming, the bubble is thick, and it's highly cyclical, making a collapse imminent. Money earned through exchanges and hedge funds is ultimately wealth floating on a bubble, unable to withstand any storm. Investing in Anthropic is like buying a hedging policy for crypto assets. AI is a globally recognized core technology, with favorable policies and strong growth certainty. Investing quick crypto profits in long-term technology sectors can both diversify the systemic risks of a single industry and allow one's capital portfolio to expand beyond the narrow confines of the crypto sphere. Top players understand the importance of being prepared for unforeseen circumstances and use cross-industry investments to mitigate bubble risks. **Irresistible Dirty Money** But Dario Amodei wasn't a fool. He saw enough warning signs from SBF, so he set up a firewall: SBF received shares without voting rights, completely isolating it from the board and corporate governance. So why, knowing the danger, did he still swallow this drastic measure? In the AI ​​startup wasteland of early 2022, a believer who could write a check for $500 million at once was an irresistible lifeline. This money was the hard currency for purchasing exorbitantly priced GPUs and assembling a top-tier team. SBF's entire narrative—rapidly accumulating wealth in the gray areas of crypto to fund the grand philanthropy of secure AI—is an extreme example of this logic. Before the crimes were exposed, it appeared to be a radical yet legitimate philosophical choice. Anthropic's acceptance of the money, in a sense, was tacit approval of this logic. They used governance firewalls to isolate the risks of the methods, but took the fuel for the consequences. In business history, many great beginnings have been accompanied by controversies surrounding the original sin of their initial capital. But this time, the controversy came too quickly and too violently. Seven months later, the FTX empire collapsed within days, and SBF's methods were proven to be outright fraud. That $500 million investment was frozen as embezzled assets. During the lengthy bankruptcy liquidation, this portion of equity was divided and auctioned off, ultimately recovering $1.34 billion for FTX's creditors. $1.34 billion, a figure that brought some comfort to the creditors. But what if the liquidation team had waited another two years? What if they hadn't been in a hurry to cash out, and had held on until Anthropic completed its $380 billion Series G funding round? That figure would have exceeded $30 billion. Between $1.34 billion and $30 billion lies a $28.66 billion opportunity cost difference. It became the largest wisp of illusory smoke on the ruins of FTX, a parallel universe dream that could have existed. This adds a layer of absurd tragedy to the fraud, taking on a financial dimension. Even more ironically, one of the buyers who participated in the auction and acquired these shares was Jane Street, SBF's former employer. History, in this way, completed a cruel cycle. A check folds into two universes. The two protagonists of the story went to opposite ends of the earth. SBF, in prison, calculates his earliest release date in 2049. The very means he used to practice his philosophy ultimately consumed him. Meanwhile, Anthropic, with that controversial initial funding, coupled with countless clean and massive investments afterward, has positioned itself as one of the rule-makers of the AI ​​era. It stands on the stage negotiating with the Pentagon, attempting to draw a line for the safety of AI. They both originated from the same intellectual soil: a rational fanaticism that willingly tolerated unconventional methods to cope with extreme uncertainty and grand goals. SBF turned unconventional methods into crimes, while Anthropic, within the red lines, built a $380 billion empire with its grand goals. Faith can drive cutting-edge creation, but it can also package the most greedy plunder. This is perhaps the coldest revelation this $500 million ill-gotten investment leaves for business history: they sometimes share the same discourse, and even share the same check in their early stages. The difference lies not in the starting point, but in the unwavering reverence for that insurmountable bottom line. That check folded into two worlds.

data.title: Multicoin: Breakdown of 4 Modes and 6 Categories of RWA On-Chain Paths
data.description:
Source: Shayon Sengupta & Spencer Applebaum, Multicoin Capital; Compiled by: Jinse Finance

This is the third article in our series on on-chain markets. The first article focused on application controlled execution (ACE) and its new ways of creating value for tokens; the second article explored adverse selection problem and prevention models in decentralized finance (DeFi).

For most of its development, DeFi activity has been largely confined to the crypto ecosystem, exhibiting self-referentiality and endogenous characteristics. People trade crypto assets, borrow crypto assets using other crypto assets as collateral, and construct derivatives based on crypto asset prices. Even the largest on-chain markets, such as spot decentralized exchanges (DEXs), lending platforms, and perpetual contracts, focus on a small number of native tokens. This wasn't intentional, but rather a consequence of the early limitations of DeFi, which restricted access to on-chain assets. This constraint has profoundly influenced the development path of DeFi. Liquidity for long-tail tokens has accumulated through Automated Market Makers (AMMs), with the industry iterating around AMM market structures for years. This model is valuable for intra-crypto asset trading, but may not be suitable for Real-World Assets (RWAs)—RWAs have drastically different liquidity management, price discovery, and risk management methods, and market makers are willing to offer narrow spreads. The lending sector follows a similar logic. Platforms like Aave and Compound require borrowers to provide collateral far exceeding the loan amount and employ strict liquidation mechanisms, disallowing margin calls (due to the high volatility of crypto assets, lack of cash flow generation, and lack of legal protection). These characteristics are almost entirely irrelevant to the vast majority of RWAs. Paul Atkins, Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), has repeatedly stated that the U.S. financial market and the broader financial system are preparing to go blockchain through tokenization, predicting that this shift (including assets such as stocks, bonds, and derivatives) could occur "within two years." We believe the next wave of DeFi growth will be driven by RWAs—where RWAs refer to assets exogenous to the crypto ecosystem. Traditional finance (TradFi) sees trillions of dollars in daily trading volume in forex, interest rates, stocks, and commodities, a figure far smaller than the crypto market. We believe that blockchain tracks are more efficient at trading these assets (more on this below) and can create a single hub allowing users to trade any asset at low cost, globally, 24/7, with robust settlement guarantees. A Brief Historical Review Since the advent of cryptocurrencies, the vision of bringing traditional financial assets onto the blockchain has been a recurring theme. The notion that assets such as stocks, credit, commodities, and real estate are "about to be on-chain" seems to have been circulating for a decade. The first wave of large-scale RWA experiments occurred between 2016 and 2018. Projects such as Polymath, Harbor, OpenFinance Network, and Neufund attempted to tokenize real estate and securities directly on the blockchain. While the idea was feasible, the timing was premature: stablecoins were not yet widespread, on-chain liquidity was limited, trading platforms were still under development, and there was a lack of clear regulation for issuance, custody, and secondary market trading. Ultimately, most of these platforms collapsed, transformed, or became irrelevant. Today, the landscape has changed dramatically. Stablecoins have become widely used settlement assets, the microstructure of on-chain markets is constantly improving and trading volume is growing significantly, institutional custody and compliance infrastructure are in place, and regulatory agencies are opening the door to experimentation. In the United States, the GENIUS Act and the CLARITY Act demonstrate that the current regulatory system is welcoming and friendly to the industry, which has led to an explosive growth in RWA activities:

Source: rwa.xyz

Two Core Variables for RWA On-Chain: Rights and Settlement

Before analyzing which asset classes are most likely to be on-chain and how, we first clarify two key variables of RWA: 1) Rights; 2) Settlement.

The first variable is the rights attached to holding the token. In some cases, tokens do not grant holders a direct claim to the asset itself, but only provide economic exposure (e.g., synthetic assets). These tokens may track stock or commodity prices, but holders have no legal claim to the real asset, unlike physically settled derivatives. In other cases, tokens represent a contractual claim to a type of off-chain asset. Many tokenized government bonds adopt this model: tokens represent claims against funds, special purpose vehicles (SPVs), or issuers holding real-world off-chain assets. In extreme cases, tokens represent direct legal ownership of the asset itself. In this case, the token is equivalent to a digital certificate of ownership of the asset, and the blockchain serves as the official registration system for the asset. The second variable is the settlement location of the asset. Currently, many RWAs only use the blockchain for recording and distribution; the real assets are still settled through the traditional financial system. For example, tokenized government bond funds may issue tokens on-chain, but the actual government bonds are held by a custodian and settled in the conventional manner. In other cases, the assets themselves can be settled directly on-chain. Synthetic perpetual contracts are a typical example of this model: when a perpetual contract is traded on an on-chain platform, the collateral is transferred directly between long and short positions. This difference is crucial because settlement determines where the core records are stored. If settlement is off-chain, the token is merely a "wrapper" of the traditional asset; if settlement is on-chain, the blockchain becomes the core settlement layer. A recent article on the X platform explored the potential drawbacks of off-chain settlement, especially in various RWA cyclic staking scenarios. The core argument is that investors need to redeem liquidity when exiting, and settlement delays may cause users to miss specific opportunities. Currently, this issue is usually handled by vault managers, but their funding costs erode returns. Some assets, once on-chain, may grant users strong legal rights, but settlement remains off-chain; others may only offer synthetic exposure but are settled entirely on-chain. In the future, we may see models where both rights and settlement are fully on-chain. Understanding these variables is crucial because they explain why different RWAs will have different on-chain paths. Each type of RWA may require its own legal structure, settlement process, and liquidity solutions, thus the on-chain approach will be customized. Furthermore, the RWA model may face significant regulatory impacts. The SEC commented on tokenized security models in a consultation paper in January. The SEC and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) may issue rules for different forms of tokenized financial instruments, and their design decisions will affect the circulation, settlement, holder whitelisting, and transfer of rights of these assets under federal and state legal frameworks. This article will analyze various on-chain models of RWAs, explain why different asset classes follow different paths along these dimensions, and explore the path dependency effect.

Four Modes of RWA On-Chain

There is currently no clear standard for RWA on-chain implementation. The industry has conducted extensive experiments around scenarios such as perpetual contracts, prediction markets, escrow packaging, and initial public offerings. Below, we categorize these methods into four types and briefly explain the advantages and disadvantages of each mode.

Mode 1: Synthetic Derivatives

Synthetic derivatives are ubiquitous in traditional financial markets because many traders are more concerned with gaining asset exposure than actually holding assets. If a trader wants to speculate on Apple, they usually don't directly buy AAPL stock, but rather seek simple exposure, efficient leverage, and a clear exit mechanism. The same applies to scenarios such as retail investors speculating on gold prices, government bond trading desks hedging foreign exchange risks, and pension funds adjusting duration.

Source: Bank for International Settlements (BIS)

In the crypto space, synthetic assets allow users to track the price of RWA without actually holding it. Protocols establish derivative contracts (usually perpetual or forward contracts) and obtain external price data through oracles. Trader positions are backed by collateralized pools, and all transactions are settled on-chain (usually in stablecoins or other crypto assets).

Typical examples include RWA perpetual contracts launched by Hyperliquid, Ostium, and Lighter, as well as prediction markets like Kalshi that provide binary outcomes of real-world events.

Both of these markets are currently experiencing extremely rapid growth: [Image of monthly nominal trading volume in the forecast market. Source: Dune, @datadashboards] [Image of RWA perpetual contract trading volume by category. Source: Dune, @datadashboards] Source: Dune, @yandhii

Based on our experience, the core advantage of synthetic derivatives is that they can be traded anytime, anywhere—no need to wait for the market to open, no intermediaries, and all transactions can be settled instantly. However, there are some trade-offs: traders do not actually hold any tangible assets, so they cannot obtain voting rights and dividends; users need to trust the accuracy of price data; funding rates for perpetual contracts may erode returns; and as a relatively new financial innovation, its regulatory rules are still in their infancy.

Model Two: Wrapped Assets (Custody Model)

In addition to synthetic assets, the wrapped asset model is becoming increasingly common in the crypto space. Its operation is simple: a regulated entity (fund, SPV, trust, etc.) purchases and holds RWA off-chain, and then issues credential tokens representing a partial claim to that asset to end users. Token holders can redeem the tokens for the underlying asset or equivalent cash, but are usually subject to redemption windows, minimum redemption amounts, and KYC (Know Your Customer) requirements.

This type of model mainly includes three forms: direct custody (Dinari's tokenized shares), pooled fund units (Ondo's OUSG, Franklin Templeton's BENJI), and securitized asset pools (Centrifuge's invoice financing, Goldfinch's credit pool). Model Three: Collateralized Borrowing This model does not directly tokenize assets, but uses off-chain RWA as collateral to obtain on-chain debt. Borrowers can pledge RWA such as real estate, corporate credit, and invoices in exchange for stablecoins. Kamino's collaboration with Anchorage and Sky's RWA vault are examples of this type. Figure Markets focuses on Home Equity Credit Lines (HELOC). We believe this model is valuable for debt financing, but the collateral lacks composability in DeFi scenarios outside of native applications. This model does not require full tokenization and supports risk stratification. However, drawbacks include a complex legal structure, liquidation requiring court intervention rather than on-chain automatic execution, over-collateralization requirements, and complex risk assessments. Furthermore, collateral lacks composability within the DeFi ecosystem.

Model Four: On-Chain Initial Offering

Unlike packaging existing assets, the issuer directly creates new securities on the blockchain. The token itself is a security, not a derivative or packaging tool. Importantly, the blockchain serves as the official ledger, and transfer restrictions and compliance requirements can be enforced through smart contracts.

This approach requires no packaging layer, supports efficient equity structure management, real-time settlement, and programmable compliance. However, each issuance requires regulatory approval, applies only to new securities, and transfer restrictions reduce DeFi composability.

Currently, crypto-native projects (such as DATs) and some large centralized finance (CeFi) companies have conducted initial experiments upon listing, and we expect such cases to increase in the future.

We believe this is the purest crypto-native RWA model and the ultimate goal of the industry.

Perhaps one day, not only will asset issuance be completed on-chain, but settlement will also be achieved on-chain through stablecoins.

Deconstructing RWA: On-Chain Paths for Different Asset Classes

In our view, the industry's homogenization of "RWA" as a single category intuitively implies that assets such as stocks, foreign exchange, credit, commodities, government bonds, real estate, private lending, and money markets will be on-chain through the same mechanism and within the same timeframe. However, we believe the reality is not so simple.

In fact, each asset class has unique requirements in terms of settlement, custody, liquidity, regulation, transparency, and listing functionality. These differences will determine the timing and method of an asset's eventual on-chain entry, which on-chain model will prevail, and where liquidity will be formed.

Below, we analyze the possible on-chain paths for various assets one by one: 1. Treasury Bonds and Money Market Funds While Treasury bonds and money market funds may be the least talked-about category in RWA, they have become the dominant sector in the RWA field. Most on-chain money market funds today adopt a variant of Mode 2 (packaged assets), especially the pooled fund share model. Popular examples include Franklin Templeton (BENJI) and Ondo (OUSG). This model is feasible for three reasons: First, Treasury bonds can only be held through the Federal Reserve's book-entry system; the fund structure achieves economies of scale, meaning that even a narrow spread can guarantee economic efficiency; second, the regulatory path is very clear through the existing money market fund regulatory framework. Looking ahead, we expect that Treasury bonds will not surpass Mode 2 (packaged assets) in the short term. The first on-chain issuance would require the US Treasury to issue bonds directly on-chain, but given the deep-rooted nature of the existing infrastructure, this is unlikely in the next few years. More realistically, Model 2 (packaged assets) products may become more efficient, more combinable, and more widely used in DeFi. 2. Private Lending Private lending has become the second fastest-growing RWA category after government bonds and money market funds. The mainstream approach is Model 2 (a variant of packaged asset securitization pools), with representative projects including Centrifuge, Credix, and Goldfinch. These protocols typically build credit asset pools and issue tokenized tiered products. Additionally, Model 3 (collateralized lending) also has some applications—institutional borrowers use off-chain lending instruments as collateral to obtain on-chain stablecoin loans (such as Sky's RWA vault). Private lending is well-suited to on-chain models because it is less regulated and more decentralized than public securities, and there is no centralized infrastructure to contend with—simply replacing bilateral legal agreements with smart contracts. Model 2 (packaged assets), through asset pools and a tiered structure, is suitable for small borrowers and consumer/SME lending. In our observation, its efficiency is far higher than traditional securitization. Model 3 (collateralized lending) is suitable for large institutions that want to obtain on-chain liquidity without restructuring the entire loan portfolio. We expect Model 2 (packaged assets) to dominate the consumer and SME lending market, and may evolve towards Model 4 (initial offering), where loans are initiated directly on-chain rather than through packaging. 3. Stocks Publicly available stocks are mainly on-chain through two models: Model 1 (synthetic derivatives) and Model 2 (packaged assets). Stock synthetic perpetual contracts have seen explosive growth in the past year. Platforms such as Hyperliquid, Ostium, and Lighter offer perpetual contracts for major stocks, tracking prices through oracles and settling in stablecoins. Traders do not need brokerage accounts, are not restricted by trading hours, and can access stock exposure 24/7. More noteworthy is the emergence of pre-IPO synthetic trading – if you believe that the private valuations of companies like Perplexity and Anthropic are too high, you can short them (however, collateral requirements, funding rates, resolution mechanisms, and payment structures vary depending on the contract and platform).

HIP-3 trading volume Source: loris.tools

Packaging models also exist, but are less attractive. Dinari tokenizes actual stocks through a broker-dealer structure, with tokens representing real shares held in custody, granting holders ownership (dividends, voting rights), but also incurring the friction costs of traditional finance (KYC, geographical restrictions, trading hours, T+1 settlement). This model is suitable for investors who want real ownership, but its scale is far smaller than synthetic assets. The New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) and Nasdaq have both stated their plans to promote more on-chain transactions through partners such as LayerZero and Kraken. The fourth model for stocks (Initial Public Offering) means that companies will issue shares directly on-chain, using tokens as official share registration certificates. Galaxy's collaboration with Superstate and Figure's exploration in the OPEN project are early examples. However, for large-scale application, listed companies rely on mature systems such as Depository Trust and Clearing Corporations (DTCCs), transfer agents, and existing stock exchanges. The relevant laws regarding shareholder rights, proxy voting, and corporate governance are also based on traditional share registration ledgers. Therefore, the company laws of core countries need to be amended for listed companies to achieve this transformation. We believe that in the short term, more realistic opportunities lie in private companies (especially startups). Private equity and venture capital equity structures are often disorganized, costly, and require intermediaries like Carta. Startups can issue equity directly on-chain, with transfer rules enforced through smart contracts, and equity structures updated automatically. Platforms like Securitize are building the necessary infrastructure. Furthermore, employees of unlisted unicorn companies typically hold illiquid equity. Some experiments already allow these employees to borrow money using their equity as collateral – we believe this is more practical and addresses a real pain point than directly tokenizing equity (which raises securities compliance issues). Some platforms are exploring this area, but the legal complexities related to employee equity restrictions remain a significant challenge. 4. Commodities The on-chain path for commodities is similar to that of stocks, primarily driven by Model 1 (synthetic derivatives). Crypto derivatives platforms widely offer perpetual contracts for commodities such as gold, silver, and oil, and have begun to achieve product-market fit. Hyperliquid's HIP-3 market saw explosive growth in commodity trading volume, with total open interest reaching a record $1.34 billion. Oil (CL), gold, and silver, along with stocks, were among the most traded contracts. Crude oil (CL-USDC) trading volume peaked at over $1-1.6 billion per 24 hours, with open interest of approximately $170-195 million. Gold and silver also saw high trading activity.

Source: Allium

Users of synthetic derivatives are mainly divided into two categories: crypto-native traders (seeking price exposure and leverage, similar to users of stock perpetual contracts); and companies such as mining companies, energy companies, and agricultural producers that need to hedge against commodity price fluctuations. For these companies, on-chain perpetual contracts are more efficient than traditional futures—they can be settled instantly via stablecoins, do not require opening a margin account on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) or the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), and are globally available without intermediaries.

Mode Two (Wrapped Assets) is suitable for some commodities, especially precious metals.

For example, tokens like Pax Gold (PAXG) and Tether Gold (XAUT) are backed by real gold stored in vaults. This model is feasible for gold (easy to store and resistant to deterioration), but less practical for most other commodities—oil, natural gas, agricultural products, and industrial metals have high storage costs, are prone to deterioration or degradation, and have complex delivery requirements, making synthetic derivatives more advantageous. The key question is whether Model Four (Initial Offering) is feasible. In practice, this requires migrating commodity ownership registration systems to the blockchain, and requiring storage receipts, bills of lading, and ownership certificates to become blockchain-native and legally valid credentials. Some projects are exploring applications related to commodities and metals in emerging markets (such as Uranium Digital), but this primarily focuses on supply chain and trade finance rather than trading scenarios. Citi's recent bill of exchange tokenization project on Solana is a typical example. We believe that for actual hedging and speculation, synthetic derivatives will remain dominant because companies require cash settlement rather than physical delivery. In addition, some digitally native commodities are emerging, such as block space, bandwidth, and computing power. These assets are fundamentally different from physical commodities; they are native to digital infrastructure and can be settled entirely on-chain. Computing power markets such as Akash and io.net tokenize GPU time; decentralized storage networks such as Filecoin and Arweave commoditize storage; DoubleZero and Pipe commoditize bandwidth; and Fuse commoditizes the energy consumption of edge nodes. These assets are naturally suited to Mode 4 (Initial Offering), requiring no physical custody. With the growth in demand for AI computing power and the maturation of Web3 infrastructure, we expect these assets to grow significantly. In summary, synthetic derivatives will continue to dominate the physical commodity market and are expected to attract real hedging funds from commodity producers and consumers; Mode 2 is suitable for easily stored commodities such as gold; Mode 4 may emerge in supply chain scenarios, but its application in financial transactions outside of digital commodities is limited. 5. Foreign Exchange Currently, most on-chain forex trading uses Mode 1 (synthetic derivatives), and this is unlikely to change in the short term. Stablecoins have made fast settlement of major currencies simple and inexpensive—fiat-backed stablecoins such as USDC, USDT, and EURC are essentially digital alternatives to the US dollar and euro, and can be settled instantly on-chain. To exchange currencies, simply exchange stablecoins on a DEX or trade synthetic forex pairs on a derivatives platform. The traditional forex market has a daily trading volume exceeding $7 trillion, mainly because cross-border payments and currency exchanges through banks are slow and expensive. On-chain systems significantly reduce these hassles. For example, Brazilian companies can hold USDC and quickly exchange it for EURC when they need euros to pay European suppliers, without using the traditional forex system. There is an interesting and largely untapped opportunity in the long-tail currency space. While US dollar and euro stablecoins are quite mature, most emerging market currencies still lack sufficiently liquid on-chain versions. Demand for stablecoins in currencies such as the Brazilian Real, Mexican Peso, Indian Rupee, and Nigerian Naira is growing. If these stablecoins can achieve sufficient liquidity, the on-chain forex market could begin to compete with traditional foreign exchange in the cross-border payments sector of developing economies. However, many emerging market countries face capital controls or regulatory restrictions, making it difficult to launch compliant fiat-backed stablecoins. Therefore, there are currently experiments exploring synthetic solutions, such as algorithmic stablecoins or overcollateralized models, that can track the prices of these currencies without direct fiat currency reserves. In the future, if governments relax regulations, we may see an on-chain forex spot market achieving liquidity comparable to bank forex trading desks or over-the-counter dealers through fiat-backed emerging market stablecoins. Teams like Hibachi and OpenFX are working towards this vision. Synthetic perpetual contracts are becoming increasingly popular in leveraged forex trading. Some platforms now offer perpetual contracts for currency pairs such as USD/JPY and EUR/GBP, settled in stablecoin cash. This allows traders to gain forex exposure without opening an account with a Contracts for Difference (CFD) broker – these brokers typically charge exorbitant fees and arbitrarily restrict withdrawals. In our view, Mode Two (asset packaging) is less meaningful for forex, as stablecoins are essentially packaged fiat currency. Circle holds US dollar reserves and issues USDC, whose functional structure is identical to packaged government bonds, only replacing bonds with cash. Mode Four (Initial Issuance) implies the use of Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs). Many central banks are testing CBDCs, but most are permissioned and poorly compatible with DeFi. Even with the official launch of CBDCs, we believe third-party issued stablecoins will still be favored by crypto users – due to their ease of use and potentially fewer restrictions. In summary, thanks to stablecoins and synthetic assets, on-chain forex trading of major currencies is essentially "solved." The core opportunity now is to expand spot trading to less common emerging market currencies, achievable through compliant fiat-backed stablecoins or synthetic solutions. This could simplify remittance processes, for example, allowing users to trade HKD/PHP directly without first converting it to USD. 6. Real Estate Real estate is one of the most difficult major asset classes to tokenize, and despite years of market enthusiasm, progress has been slow. Currently, the two most common approaches are Mode 1 (synthetic derivatives) and Mode 2 (packaged assets). Synthetic solutions include platforms like Parcl and PricedOut, which offer perpetual contracts referencing the median price in a specific region as a market index; in the packaged model, real estate is incorporated into an SPV or Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT), with tokens representing partial ownership. Platforms like RealT and Lofty allow investors to purchase tokens linked to individual properties or real estate funds to capture rental income and asset appreciation. Compared to other RWAs, real estate tokenization faces some unique challenges: properties are difficult to sell quickly, cannot be easily divided, transaction costs are high, they are subject to local regulations, require continuous maintenance, and ownership legal relationships are complex. Even with tokenization of apartment buildings, property management, tenant affairs, maintenance, tax payments, and compliance with local regulations remain essential – issues that blockchain-based ownership registration cannot resolve. While tokens are easy to trade, transferring the property itself remains extremely difficult. Mode Three (mortgage lending) has made some progress. Platforms like Figure Markets and some Sky Vaults allow users to use real estate as collateral for on-chain loans, typically Home Equity Credit Lines (HELOCs). This model works because the property remains off-chain (the borrower continues to reside there), and the protocol only obtains a lien; if the borrower defaults, the property will be liquidated through conventional foreclosure procedures. In our view, the ideal scenario is Mode Four (Initial Offering) – where property ownership itself is recorded on-chain, and transfers are completed via smart contracts. This would eliminate title insurance, escrow services, and the weeks-long closing process. Some countries with less developed property registration systems (parts of Latin America and Africa) are testing blockchain-based land registration systems. However, in developed markets like the US, property law is deeply entrenched at the state and local levels. Migrating centuries of property records and legal precedents to the blockchain requires the cooperation of thousands of jurisdictions. We believe that real estate will currently primarily employ Model Two (asset packaging) to attract investors seeking partial ownership of specific properties. Model Three (mortgage lending) helps unlock the liquidity of existing properties, but Model Four (direct issuance) may still be several years away in developed markets. The core challenge is that real estate tokenization does not solve the inherent illiquidity of the asset; it merely adds a layer of liquidity on top of the illiquid asset. **Summary** While the crypto industry has lumped all RWAs together and widely disseminated this view, causing some confusion, each asset class is actually unique, and the on-chain process may vary: 1. Government bonds have been massively on-chained through packaged fund structures, and this model is unlikely to change in the short term (due to the deeply ingrained infrastructure of native issuance); 2. Private lending is divided into two models: securitized asset pools and collateralized loans. As protocols mature, it may evolve towards native on-chain issuance; 3. Currently, public stock trading is mainly based on synthetic assets, with packaging models having some application; the main opportunity in the private market is to unlock liquidity for unicorn company employees and simplify equity structure tables; we believe RWAs... The grandest vision may be the direct on-chain issuance of publicly traded stocks; 4. Commodity price exposure and hedging will primarily be achieved through synthetic assets, but digital commodities such as computing power and storage are new categories that can be settled directly on-chain; 5. Foreign exchange has largely achieved on-chain trading through stablecoins, with the core opportunity lying in long-tail emerging market currencies; 6. Real estate remains the most challenging market because tokenization cannot solve the fundamental illiquidity problem of physical assets. Path dependence is extremely important in the RWA field. Assets with deeply entrenched settlement infrastructure (government bonds, publicly traded stocks) will continue to use the packaged model for many years to come; while assets with fragmented markets and relationship-driven dependencies (private lending, private equity) can leap to the on-chain model more quickly; truly new digital commodities can achieve native on-chain performance from their inception.

data.title: Saylor's New Strategy for Buying Bitcoin
data.description:
Author: thedefinvestor Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Bitcoin has performed quite well in the past few weeks. Admittedly, it has fallen from $74,000 to $69,000. But overall, since the outbreak of the US-Iran conflict, Bitcoin has outperformed stocks and gold on most trading days.

I believe one key reason is the massive buying pressure from Strategy recently.

In the past 19 days alone, Strategy, the world's largest Bitcoin treasury company led by Michael Saylor, has bought over $3 billion worth of Bitcoin. March 2026 is also expected to be the month with the largest increase in Bitcoin holdings by Strategy through STRC.

STRC is Michael Saylor's latest financial innovation, providing the funding for Strategy's massive Bitcoin buying spree. I believe it's a major driver of Bitcoin's relative strength. What exactly is STRC? STRC is a perpetual preferred stock issued by Strategy, promising high monthly dividends while maintaining a target price of $100. In a sense, it's like an interest-bearing stable asset with an annualized return of 11.5%. However, as I will explain below, it clearly carries risks. Buying STRC is equivalent to lending money to Saylor's Strategy company. The company raises funds by issuing STRC and then uses that money to buy more Bitcoin. The recent surge in STRC demand is the direct reason why Strategy has been able to buy so much BTC. How does the underlying mechanism work? As mentioned earlier, the target price for STRC is $100. All holders receive monthly dividends. When market demand is high and the STRC trading price is above $100, Strategy can issue new shares and sell them, raising more funds to buy BTC. When STRC's trading price falls below $100, Strategy can choose to increase its dividend to attract more buyers and bring the price back to par value. Initially, STRC's annualized dividend was 9%, which has now been increased to 11.5%. Simply put: Strategy borrows money from the public at a cost of 11.5% and uses that money to buy Bitcoin. Where does this money come from? A large portion comes from MSTR. When MSTR's stock price trades at a premium to the net value of its Bitcoin holdings, Strategy can issue more MSTR shares and sell them for a profit, thus obtaining another source of funding to support the entire mechanism. Initially, STRC was only listed on platforms such as Nasdaq and Robinhood. However, DeFi protocols like Apyx and Saturn have now tokenized their versions on-chain, further driving up demand. Is the STRC model truly sustainable? Strategy has approximately $2 billion in cash reserves, enough to cover all STRC dividend payments over the next 1-2 years. Therefore, the possibility of Strategy suddenly being unable to pay dividends in the short term is extremely low. However, a key point to remember is that the STRC price is not rigidly locked at $100. Even if you buy at $100, there's no guarantee you can sell at $100 later—although so far, it has always returned to that price after short-term dips. However, if Bitcoin enters a multi-year bear market, STRC demand could decline, potentially triggering a decoupling. Strategy's financial model heavily relies on MSTR maintaining a premium in its share price. Once the MSTR premium disappears, Strategy will lose a core funding channel and may be unable to pay STRC dividends without selling Bitcoin. This could put Strategy in a very unfavorable situation, so the risk certainly exists. However, for now, STRC is clearly creating considerable buying pressure on Bitcoin. If you hold BTC and are wondering why it has recently outperformed most assets, you should probably thank Michael Saylor.

data.title: From Wall Street to Hyperliquid: What Does the S&P 500 Perpetual Contract Mean?
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

On March 18, 2026, S&P Dow Jones Indices authorized the S&P 500 index for perpetual contract trading on Hyperliquid.

Affected by this positive news, the price of HYPE briefly rose to $43.51 before falling back. As of press time, it was trading at $39.95, a 7-day increase of 5%.


I. The S&P 500 Index can be traded on Hyperliquid

Standard & Poor's Dow Jones Indices has authorized Trade[XYZ] (“XYZ”) to use the S&P 500 Index to launch the first and only officially licensed perpetual derivative contract based on the S&P 500 Index.

A perpetual contract is a highly speculative derivative that never expires and has no strike price (i.e., the contract's execution point).

A perpetual contract is a highly speculative derivative that never expires and has no strike price (i.e., the contract's execution point).

Perpetual futures offer traders extremely high leverage, amplifying profits, but also the potential to wipe out the entire investment. Like other native cryptocurrency assets, perpetual contracts trade 24/7. XYZ is the first and largest real-world asset market on the Hyperliquid platform, offering perpetual derivatives trading services through Hyperliquid. Since October 2025, XYZ's market volume has exceeded $100 billion, with a current annualized trading volume exceeding $600 billion. XYZ aims to bring traditional financial assets (such as bonds, commodities, real estate, or structured products) into the blockchain ecosystem, enabling more efficient, transparent, and composable financial services. According to Trade[XYZ], traders can use a maximum leverage of 50x with this contract. Hyperliquid allows traders to leverage their investments through perpetual futures contracts backed by stablecoins (primarily USDC), enabling them to trade without opening a brokerage account or accessing regulated commodity futures exchanges like the CME Group. Hyperliquid's system consists of two parts: HyperCore and HyperEVM. HyperCore runs entirely on-chain, with spot and perpetual futures order books recording every order, trade, and settlement, almost instantly, and supporting a processing capacity of up to approximately 200,000 orders per second. Meanwhile, HyperEVM provides an Ethereum-compatible environment where developers can deploy smart contracts and build applications that interact with exchange liquidity. Unlike many decentralized exchanges that rely on automated market makers (AMMs), platforms like Hyperliquid use on-chain limit-price order books, providing professional traders with more precise pricing, smaller spreads, and familiar order types. Hyperliquid also offers sub-second final confirmation of trades, enabling faster execution speeds that better meet the needs of algorithmic and high-frequency trading strategies. Furthermore, portfolio margin allows traders to manage the risk of the entire portfolio, rather than a single position, thus improving capital efficiency. Hyperliquid thus becomes a professional exchange connecting traditional financial markets with the crypto market. II. What Does the Listing of S&P 500 Perpetual Contracts Mean? 1. The Great Convergence of DeFi and Traditional Finance The S&P 500 perpetual contracts connect DeFi with core traditional financial benchmarks in an unprecedented way. For the first time, eligible non-US investors can gain leveraged exposure to the S&P 500 index on a decentralized platform through an officially authorized, native digital product that supports 24/7 trading. Previously, retail and institutional traders relied on ETFs and centralized futures markets to invest in the S&P 500 index. 2. Crypto Investors Gain 24/7 Leveraged Exposure to the S&P 500 Index This move opens the door for non-US investors to gain 24/7 leveraged exposure to the S&P 500 index through a blockchain-based platform. Previously, major macroeconomic news was released when markets were closed on weekends, and traders typically had to guess the S&P 500's direction when the market opened on Monday. However, with these new perpetual contracts, traders can trade accurately and immediately after the news is released. When the first missile struck Iran and traditional oil markets were closed, cryptocurrency traders were able to trade oil futures on the decentralized exchange Hyperliquid. By offering licensed perpetual contracts available 24/7, Hyperliquid positions itself as an exchange where traders can react instantly to macroeconomic developments without waiting for market hours. This makes it possible to trade the S&P 500 index with on-chain leverage around the clock. 3. Providing More Arbitrage Opportunities for Professional Traders When the same index appears simultaneously on traditional exchanges and on-chain perpetual contracts, professional traders can engage in arbitrage and cross-market hedging between different markets. This helps improve price discovery efficiency, attracts more market makers and quantitative funds to the crypto market, and further increases market liquidity. 4. Paving the Way for Other Traditional Financial Benchmarks to Enter the Crypto Market The S&P 500's move pioneered the entry of traditional core financial indices into the on-chain perpetual derivatives market with official authorization. Once the market validates the feasibility of this model in terms of compliance, liquidity, and user demand, the Nasdaq index, Treasury yield indices, and others may enter the decentralized derivatives market in a similar manner. The curtain has been raised on the global capital market's shift from traditional centralized exchanges to on-chain decentralized exchanges. 5. The S&P 500 Index Offers Greater Advantages The S&P 500 Index is the core of the global trading ecosystem, with its associated futures, options, ETFs, and structured products seeing daily trading volumes exceeding $1 trillion. The expanded S&P 500 ecosystem offers key advantages including: 24/7 market access, unrestricted by traditional trading hours; expanded access for qualified participants beyond traditional exchanges; a transparent, high-performance on-chain environment; and institutional-grade index data.
